name: Lut and the overturned towns
epithet: One man against a people who called the forbidden normal

era.period: The friend of the Most Merciful
era.anchor: In the days of Ibrahim, before Ishaq was born

title: Lut and the overturned towns: a prophet who would not call the forbidden normal
meta_description: Day 14 of 29: Lut, Ibrahim's nephew, sent to a people who invented a sin no nation had committed, his patient call, the angels who came as guests, and the towns lifted and overturned. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets.
keywords: Lut, Lot, stories of the prophets, Mufti Menk, people of Sodom, halal and haram, Surah Hud, Surah al-Araf, ash-Shuara, al-Ankabut, Buruja

video.title: Stories Of The Prophets-14 - Lot [Lut] - Mufti Menk

source_note: Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets, episode 14 (Lut, peace be upon him). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is Mufti Menk's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: There was a young man who watched Ibrahim walk out of a fire unburned, and believed. When almost no one else on earth would, he did. His name was Lut, peace be upon him, the one English Bibles call Lot, and he was Ibrahim's nephew. Years later his uncle sent him north, to a cluster of towns near what we now call the Dead Sea, and there Lut found a people doing something no nation in the history of the world had done before them.

This is day fourteen of twenty-nine. It is one of the harder stories Mufti Menk tells, and he tells it with care, because the lesson lives in the hardest part: that right and wrong are not set by a crowd, and that a believer does not get to vote them away.

sections.h2: The boy who believed when no one else would

ayah.arabic: وَلُوطًا ءَاتَيْنَٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا وَنَجَّيْنَٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلَّتِى كَانَت تَّعْمَلُ ٱلْخَبَٰٓئِثَ ۗ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَٰسِقِينَ
ayah.text: And to Lot We gave judgement and knowledge, and We saved him from the city that was committing wicked deeds. Indeed, they were a people of evil, defiantly disobedient.

paras: Lut was a youngster in the days Ibrahim, peace be upon him, was thrown into the fire and came out untouched. When that happened, this young man accepted his uncle's message of the one God. Mufti Menk pauses to let the loneliness of that register: at the time there were only two who believed alongside Ibrahim, Lut and a woman named Sarah. Two people. Lut did not come to faith because everyone around him held it. He came to it against everyone around him, which is exactly the kind of man Allah would later need where He was about to send him.

Ibrahim went on north, and he sent Lut to the people of Sodom, the towns the West remembers as Sodom and Gomorrah. And when Lut arrived, Allah's own word for what he found there is not a polite one. Allah calls their deeds al-khaba'ith, the foul, the filthy, the rotten. Hold on to that, because the whole episode turns on refusing to rename it anything cleaner than what Allah named it.

sections.h2: A sin no nation had committed before

ayah.arabic: وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلْفَٰحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُم بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
ayah.text: And [We had sent] Lot when he said to his people, "Do you commit such immorality as no one has preceded you with from among the worlds [i.e., peoples]?"

paras: The men of these towns had turned away from the women Allah created as their mates and turned toward each other, openly, and it had become the custom of the place. Lut put it to them plainly, and the Qur'an preserves his words: do you approach men with desire instead of women? You are a people who have transgressed every limit. He held the mirror up: this is a thing the world has never seen until you.

Mufti Menk lingers here for a reason. Some today, he says, even claim to have studied the Qur'an and then argue it only forbids the coercion, not the act itself when both agree. He answers it from the verse itself: when Lut tells them they leave the women for the men, he is not saying go and force the women instead. The objection collapses the moment you read what Lut actually said. The deed is the deed, and Allah calls it fahishah, gross indecency, and qawmun musrifun, a people gone past all bounds. Mufti Menk also dismisses the excuse that it is something in the genes, something inherited and so unchosen: by that logic, he says, the man addicted to adultery or to drink could plead the same, and every sin on earth would dissolve into an alibi. No one is born owing this. It is a choice a heart makes and then defends.

ayah.text: Do you approach males among the worlds

ayah.arabic: وَتَذَرُونَ مَا خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُم مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُم ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ عَادُونَ
ayah.text: And leave what your Lord has created for you as mates? But you are a people transgressing."

paras: Like every prophet before him, Lut asked for nothing. When they accused him of coming to their town only to gain something from them, he answered as Nuh and Hud and Salih had answered before: I am a trustworthy messenger to you, so fear Allah and obey me, and I ask of you no wage for it, my reward is only with the Lord of the worlds. Not a coin, not a thing of gold or silver. He wanted their souls, not their pockets.

And the sin was never only the one. The Qur'an names the rest: they were highwaymen who lay in wait for the traveller and robbed him on the road, and in their gathering places, their clubs, they would meet to cheer one another on in evil and commit it in the open. A whole society had organised itself around indecency and made it the entertainment of the town. Lut stood in the middle of it, alone, calling them back, year after year, while they laughed and called him mad.

sections.h2: When the forbidden becomes the law of the land

ayah.arabic: وَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ أَخْرِجُوهُم مِّن قَرْيَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ
ayah.text: But the answer of his people was only that they said, "Evict them from your city! Indeed, they are men who keep themselves pure."

paras: Notice what their society had become, because this is the part that should reach across the centuries and touch you. They had so completely inverted right and wrong that purity itself was now the offence. Throw these people out, they said, they keep themselves clean. Being decent was the crime. They had even passed a rule among themselves that Lut was not allowed to host a single guest, because a guest was prey. Wrong was not just permitted; it was protected by law, and right was the thing you got punished for.

Here Mufti Menk turns it on us, and so will we. As Muslims we are not free, and we say that as a boast, not a complaint. We chose to surrender, and the day you surrender you accept a code. He gives the image of citizenship: live in a country and you cannot simply break its laws and stay a citizen in good standing. Islam is like that. You may sin and stay a Muslim, weak and in need of repentance, but the moment you take a thing Allah forbade and rebrand it as permitted, you have tried to edit the law you swore to keep. This is the heart of halal and haram: they are Allah's to define, not the crowd's, not the era's, not the syllabus a society decides to teach its children. A whole nation calling something normal does not move it one inch on Allah's scale. The people of Lut are the proof, preserved in the Qur'an forever, that the majority can be wrong about everything that matters.

sections.h2: I detest your deeds, and a du'a in the open

ayah.text: He said, "Indeed, I am, toward your deed, of those who detest [it].
My Lord, save me and my family from [the consequence of] what they do."

paras: Lut never softened. I am, he told them, of those who utterly detest what you do; I want no part of it and I never will. He warned them, again and again, that a punishment was coming from Allah. And when a warner warns a people long enough and they will not bend, the Qur'an records the same chilling reply it records from other doomed nations: their only answer was to dare him, bring us the punishment of Allah, if you are telling the truth.

So Lut did the one thing a servant can always do when the whole world stands against him. He raised his hands. My Lord, save me and my family from what they do. My Lord, support me against this corrupting people. Mufti Menk asks us to feel the arithmetic of that prayer: one man, and two daughters who believed with him. Three souls in a city of thousands, and the rest of them, in the Qur'an's own description, blind, no longer able to tell right from wrong at all. When you are that outnumbered, the prophets show you the move. You stop arguing with the crowd and you start talking to the only One whose verdict will hold.

sections.h2: Guests too beautiful to be safe

ayah.arabic: وَجَآءَهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ يُهْرَعُونَ إِلَيْهِ وَمِن قَبْلُ كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ قَالَ يَٰقَوْمِ هَٰٓؤُلَآءِ بَنَاتِى هُنَّ أَطْهَرُ لَكُمْ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُخْزُونِ فِى ضَيْفِىٓ ۖ أَلَيْسَ مِنكُمْ رَجُلٌ رَّشِيدٌ
ayah.text: And his people came hastening to him, and before [this] they had been doing evil deeds. He said, "O my people, these are my daughters; they are purer for you. So fear Allāh and do not disgrace me concerning my guests. Is there not among you a man of reason?"

paras: Allah's answer to the du'a came walking into town. Three guests, in the form of strikingly handsome young men, the most beautiful anyone there had seen. They were angels, though Lut did not yet know it. His daughters saw them first and ran to their father in fear, because they knew exactly what their city would do to such guests after dark. Lut rushed the men into his house and bolted the door, hoping against hope that no one had seen, and even as he hosted them he kept telling them, with a heavy heart, how vile his people were, half as a warning to leave, half in shame at what he could not protect them from.

But his own wife betrayed him. She was not a believer, and she slipped word out to the town: three beautiful men are in Lut's house. The Qur'an pairs her elsewhere with the wife of Nuh as a warning that even a prophet's household is no guarantee of faith. The mob came running, hammering on the door, and Lut stood between them and his guests in an agony you can feel through the verse: fear Allah, do not disgrace me before my guests, is there not one right-minded man among you? He even offered them the lawful path of marriage to the women of his community, calling them my daughters. They did not want what was pure. We know, they sneered, exactly what we want. He was almost helpless. If only, he cried, I had the strength to stop you, or some mighty support to lean on.

ayah.arabic: فَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا جَعَلْنَا عَٰلِيَهَا سَافِلَهَا وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا حِجَارَةً مِّن سِجِّيلٍ مَّنضُودٍ
ayah.text: So when Our command came, We made the highest part [of the city] its lowest and rained upon them stones of layered hard clay, [which were]

paras: The mighty support he wished for was already inside the house. The guests spoke at last: O Lut, we are messengers of your Lord. They will never reach you. Take your family and leave by night, and let none of you look back, except your wife, for what strikes them will strike her. Their appointed time is the morning, and is the morning not near? So Lut left in the dark with his daughters, and the dawn came up on the towns for the last time.

Mufti Menk walks the punishment through in three blows, each one named in the Qur'an. First a single, shattering cry at sunrise, a sound so vast it shook them where they stood. Then the angel Jibril, peace be upon him, returned to his true form, the form the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ described as having six hundred wings, and with the tip of one wing he reached beneath the whole settlement, lifted it high into the sky, and turned it over, so that the top became the bottom and the bottom the top. And then, as the wreckage fell, came the third blow: a rain of stones of baked clay, each one marked, falling in order, one after another, until in moments nothing of that people was left. One of those stones was for the wife who looked back.

sections.h2: A sign left on a road still travelled

ayah.arabic: قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ مُّجْرِمِينَ
ayah.text: They said, "Indeed, we have been sent to a people of criminals
Marked in the presence of your Lord for the transgressors."

paras: Before they ever reached Sodom, those same angels had stopped with Ibrahim, who served them a roasted calf and grew afraid when they would not eat. They told him his wife would bear a son, Ishaq, peace be upon him, and they told him their errand. Ibrahim, ever tender, began to plead for Lut's people, until he was told gently to let it go: the decree had been sealed. And the angels named their cargo, the verses Mufti Menk loves to recite from the opening of the twenty-seventh part of the Qur'an: we are sent to a criminal people, to send down on them stones of clay, marked by your Lord for those who broke all bounds. When they reached the town they found, as they said, but one house of those who had surrendered to Allah, and they brought its people out. Lut, peace be upon him, returned to his uncle to tell him what had happened, and was amazed to find Ibrahim already knew.

Then Allah did something with the ruin. He left it. He says elsewhere that the wreck of those towns lies on a road that people still pass, by day and by night, so that travellers who fear a painful punishment would see it and take heed. Mufti Menk's point lands hard for us: in Lut's day this evil was one small pocket of the earth, and Allah overturned it. Today whole nations promote it, fund it, teach it, and reward it, and still it remains exactly what Allah called it. Perhaps, he says, it is only the handful of believers scattered among the cities that holds the punishment back. The Qur'an keeps Lut's towns in front of us for the same reason it kept the rubble visible: so that no believer is ever fooled into thinking that when the whole world renames a sin, the sin has changed. It has not. Allah's halal and haram outlast every empire that votes against them.

dua.translit: Rabbi najjini wa ahli mimma ya'malun
dua.en: My Lord, save me and my family from [the consequence of] what they do. (Lut's du'a, Surah ash-Shu'ara 26:169)

lessons_intro: Lut stood almost entirely alone, and held. These threads run straight out of Mufti Menk's telling.

lessons.bold: Halal and haram are Allah's to define.
lessons.text: Not the crowd's, not the era's, not a syllabus. A whole nation calling a sin normal does not move it on Allah's scale. The people of Lut are the proof, kept in the Qur'an forever.

lessons.bold: Surrender means you are no longer free.
lessons.text: You may sin and stay a Muslim, weak and repenting. But to rebrand the forbidden as permitted is to edit the law you swore to keep. That is the line Lut would not cross.

lessons.bold: Truth is not measured by the count.
lessons.text: Three believers stood against a city of thousands, and the three were right. When you are outnumbered for the truth, you are in the company of the prophets.

lessons.bold: Don't call your sin your nature.
lessons.text: No one is born owing a sin. The 'it's in my genes' excuse, Mufti Menk notes, would absolve every wrong on earth. A heart chooses, then defends the choice. Repentance is always open.

lessons.bold: When the world stands against you, raise your hands.
lessons.text: Lut stopped arguing with the mob and turned to his Lord: save me and my family. That move is always available, no matter how badly you are outnumbered.

faq.q: Who was Lut, and how was he related to Ibrahim?
faq.a: Lut, peace be upon him (the Lot of English Bibles), was Ibrahim's nephew. He was a young man when Ibrahim was thrown into the fire and came out unharmed, and he was among the very first to believe in Ibrahim's message, alongside Sarah, when almost no one else on earth did. Ibrahim later sent him as a prophet to the people of Sodom.

faq.q: Where in the Qur'an is the story of Lut told?
faq.a: It runs across several surahs, and Mufti Menk draws from them all: Surah Hud 11:77-83, Surah al-A'raf 7:80-84, Surah al-Hijr 15:57-77, Surah ash-Shu'ara 26:160-175, and Surah al-Ankabut 29:28-35, with the angels' errand recited in Surah adh-Dhariyat 51:31-37. Allah calls their deeds al-khaba'ith, the foul deeds (Surah al-Anbiya 21:74).

faq.q: Did Lut really offer his daughters to the mob?
faq.a: When Lut said 'these are my daughters, they are purer for you' (Surah Hud 11:78), the scholars Mufti Menk follows understand him to be pointing the men toward the lawful path of marriage to the women of his community, whom a prophet would treat as his own daughters, away from the indecency they were rushing toward. It is a desperate plea to turn them back to what Allah made permitted, not an offer of his own children to be wronged.

faq.q: Doesn't the Bible accuse Lut himself of wrongdoing?
faq.a: Mufti Menk addresses this directly. The altered scriptures contain a story blaming Lut for an act with his own daughters. In Islam this is rejected outright as blasphemy against a prophet. Allah chose His messengers from the very best and purest of people; Lut, peace be upon him, is free of it. Mufti Menk notes we neither affirm nor deny details that reach us only from those scriptures unless the Qur'an confirms them, but a slander against a prophet is something the Qur'an's whole portrait of the prophets denies.

faq.q: How does this connect to me and to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ?
faq.a: Lut's stand is the truth at the centre of halal and haram: right and wrong are Allah's to set, and no majority can vote them away. That is a pillar of your own faith. And Lut is one more rehearsal of the patience the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ would later show in Makkah, a lone caller mocked by his own people for refusing to bless what they loved. The same angel, Jibril, who carried Allah's word to Muhammad ﷺ is the one who overturned these towns.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: Lut, peace be upon him, spent a lifetime as one voice in a place that had made wrong its law and called purity a crime, and he never once renamed the sin to make it easier to live beside. He lost his wife to it and almost lost his guests to it, and still he warned, and still he prayed, and in the end it was the towns that were overturned and his name that Allah kept. The Qur'an left their rubble on a travelled road on purpose, so that you would pass it and remember.

Carry the one thing he carried. When the world around you decides a thing Allah forbade is now normal, good, even something to be celebrated and taught, the world has not changed the law; it has only chosen the wrong side of it. So hold the line gently and without apology, and when you feel outnumbered, do what Lut did. O Allah, save us and our families from what the heedless do, keep our hearts on what You made pure when everyone around us calls it old-fashioned, support us against every corrupting tide, and gather us with Your prophets and Your final Messenger ﷺ in the home You kept for those who would not bow to the crowd. Ameen.

related.label: Halal and haram
related.note: A people who called the forbidden normal, and the prophet who would not

slides:
Day 14 of 29.

A young man watched Ibrahim walk out of a fire unburned, and believed when almost no one on earth would.

His name was Lut. Lot, in English. Ibrahim's nephew.

Ibrahim sent him north, to the towns near the Dead Sea.

There Lut found a people doing a thing no nation in history had ever done.

Allah's own word for it: al-khaba'ith. The foul deeds.

The men had left the women Allah made their mates, openly, and made it the custom of the town.

Lut held up the mirror: do you approach men with desire instead of women? You are a people gone past every limit.

Their society had inverted everything.

'Throw these people out,' they said of Lut. 'They keep themselves pure.'

Decency was the crime. They had even outlawed him from hosting a single guest.

Here is the line that reaches across the centuries:

Halal and haram are Allah's to define. Not the crowd's. Not the era's.

A whole nation calling a sin normal does not move it one inch on Allah's scale.

Allah's answer to Lut's du'a walked into town: three guests, impossibly beautiful. Angels, though he didn't know it yet.

His own wife, no believer, slipped word to the mob. They came hammering on his door.

'Fear Allah. Do not disgrace me before my guests. Is there not one right-minded man among you?'

Then the angels spoke: O Lut, we are messengers of your Lord. Leave by night. Don't look back.

At dawn: a shattering cry. Then Jibril lifted the towns on the tip of one wing and turned them over, top to bottom. Then a rain of marked stones of baked clay.

One stone was for the wife who looked back.

Allah left the ruin on a road people still travel, so we would see it and take heed.

In Lut's day it was one small place. Today whole nations promote it.

It remains exactly what Allah called it.
social.caption: Day 14 of 29: Lut, peace be upon him, Ibrahim's nephew, sent to a people who invented a sin no nation had committed and made it the law of the land. He never renamed it to make it easier to live beside. He lost his wife to it, nearly lost his angel-guests to it, and in the end it was the towns that were overturned and his name that Allah kept. The lesson at the centre: halal and haram are Allah's to define, never the crowd's, and a whole world calling a sin normal does not change it. One more rehearsal of the patience the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ would show in Makkah. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's beloved series (the episode is embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A prophet a day at buruja.com. #StoriesOfTheProphets #MuftiMenk #Lut #prophets #Quran #Buruja #islam #halalandharam

email.subject: The prophet who would not call the forbidden normal
email.alt_subject: One man, three believers, a whole city against them
email.preview: Day 14 of 29: Lut, Ibrahim's nephew, and the towns Allah turned upside down.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

There was a young man who watched Ibrahim, peace be upon him, walk out of a fire unburned, and believed when almost no one else on earth would. His name was Lut, the Lot of English Bibles, and he was Ibrahim's nephew. Ibrahim sent him north to a cluster of towns near the Dead Sea, and there Lut found a people doing something no nation in the history of the world had done before them.

The men had turned away from the women Allah created as their mates and turned toward each other, openly, until it became the custom of the place. Allah's own word for what they did is not a gentle one: al-khaba'ith, the foul deeds (Surah al-Anbiya 21:74). And the most striking thing about that society is how completely it had inverted right and wrong. When they wanted Lut gone, their reason was: 'Evict them from your city. Indeed, they are men who keep themselves pure' (Surah al-A'raf 7:82). Decency itself was the offence. They had even passed a law that Lut was forbidden to host a single guest.

Here is where Mufti Menk turns the story on us, and so does today's entry. As Muslims we are not free, and we say that as a boast. We chose to surrender, and that means we accept a code. You can sin and remain a Muslim, weak and in need of repentance. But the moment you take a thing Allah forbade and rebrand it as permitted, you have tried to edit the law you swore to keep. This is the whole heart of halal and haram: they are Allah's to define, not the crowd's, not the era's, not the syllabus a society decides to teach its children. A whole nation calling a sin normal does not move it one inch on Allah's scale.

Lut stood almost alone, three believers in a city of thousands, and held. Allah answered his du'a with three guests of impossible beauty, angels in the form of young men. His own wife, no believer, betrayed him to the mob. And at dawn the angel Jibril lifted the towns on the tip of one wing and turned them over, top to bottom, and rained down marked stones of baked clay. One was for the wife who looked back. Allah left the ruin on a road people still travel, so we would see it and remember.

Tonight, one du'a, the one Lut himself made: My Lord, save me and my family from what they do.

Read day 14, and watch Mufti Menk's full telling, at the link below.
